name: Kshemaraja
dates_notes: Direct disciple of Abhinavagupta; dated by lineage.

lineage_polemical.note: Kṣemarāja's Pratyabhijñā-Hṛdaya and Spanda-Nirṇaya rest on the Bhairava-tantra scriptural ground that the Mālinī-Vijaya-Tantra anchors; the Mālinī-Vijaya is one of the source-tantras Kṣemarāja's compact summaries presuppose, and his Svacchanda-Tantra-Uddyota develops the wider Bhairava-tantra reading.

core_thesis: Kṣemarāja, the direct disciple of Abhinavagupta, frames his own role in the *Pratyabhijñā-Hṛdaya* preamble as exposition (*samucitam*) of his teacher's *Pratyabhijñā* doctrine for those whose minds are not yet entered into the *mahāśāstra*; the Trika *Pratyabhijñā* and *Spanda* materials are condensed into compact, teaching-grade texts whose structure has shaped how the school is read since. The single reality is *Cit* — Śiva-as-consciousness — possessed of *svātantrya* (*Pratyabhijñā-Hṛdaya* sūtra 1, *citiḥ svatantrā viśvasiddhi-hetuḥ*); the universe is its own free *unmeṣa* (unfolding), and *spanda* is the unbroken pulsation by which manifestation as a whole occurs — not a single *guṇa*, but the dynamism of *prakṛti* / *mahāmāyā*-scale taken as Śiva's own pulse (*Spanda-Nirṇaya* on *Spanda-Kārikā* 1.1, *yasyonmeṣa-nimeṣābhyāṃ jagataḥ pralayodayau*). The signature architecture is *Pratyabhijñā-Hṛdaya* sūtra 4: *cit eva … saṃkucita-citta-rūpā* — "consciousness *itself*, by its own *svātantrya*, contracts itself into the limited subject (*cetana*)" (*PH* sūtra 4 with auto-*vṛtti*). Bondage is therefore Śiva's voluntary self-contraction, never an external imposition (*PH* sūtras 5–9); liberation is the recognition (*pratyabhijñā*) of one's own consciousness as that very self-contracting Śiva (*PH* sūtras 13–18). The pedagogical aim is to make this recognition operative in a single concentrated reading (*PH* preamble; *Śiva-Sūtra-Vimarśinī* on *ŚS* 1.1 *caitanyam ātmā*).

engaged_works.title: Pratyabhijna-Hrdayam
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored compact summary of Pratyabhijñā doctrine.
engaged_works.summary: Twenty short sūtras with Kṣemarāja's own *Vṛtti*, designed as a single-sitting introduction to the entire *Pratyabhijñā* system. Sūtra 1 (*citiḥ svatantrā viśvasiddhi-hetuḥ*): consciousness, by its own *svātantrya*, is the cause of the manifestation of the universe. Sūtras 2–3 (*svecchayā svabhittau viśvam unmīlayati*) elaborate the *ābhāsa*-structure of manifestation — Cit unfolds the universe "by her own will, on her own canvas (*svabhitti*)," the canvas-metaphor making explicit that there is no external substrate. Sūtra 4 — the architectural heart of the work, and the reason it is canonical for comparative non-dualism — states that the *cetana* (limited subject) is itself nothing but consciousness *that has contracted* (*saṃkucita*) by its own *svātantrya*: the limited self is the unlimited supreme self in the mode of self-imposed contraction (*PH* sūtra 4 with auto-*vṛtti*). Subsequent sūtras work out the consequences: the appearance of the world to the bound subject (*PH* sūtras 5–7), the structure of bondage as forgetfulness of one's own *cit*-nature (*PH* sūtras 8–11), the upward path of recognition (*pratyabhijñā*; *PH* sūtras 12–17), the *vikalpa-kṣaya* and consequent attainment of *cidānanda* (*PH* sūtra 17), and the residual condition of the *jīvanmukta* who acts within manifestation while knowing it as own self-display (*PH* sūtras 18–20). The contrast with Vedāntic *māyā* is precise: where Advaita treats the manifest as *adhyāsa* (superimposition, sublatable), the *PH*'s *saṃkoca*-structure treats it as Cit's own free, real, internal differentiation; bondage is therefore not error to be sublated but a self-imposed mode to be re-cognized.

engaged_works.title: Spanda-Nirnaya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on the Spanda-Kārikā.
engaged_works.summary: Kṣemarāja's mature commentary on Vasugupta/Kallaṭa's *Spanda-Kārikā* (the on-disk witness `spanda_karika_comm.txt` is the GRETIL transcription of the *Spanda-Kārikā with Kṣemarāja's commentary*, conventionally identified as the *Spanda-Nirṇaya*), the foundational text of the *Spanda* tradition. The *Nirṇaya* takes *spanda* as the pulsation of consciousness by which the entire manifest universe is continuously projected, sustained, and reabsorbed — a dynamism intrinsic to *cit* and inseparable from its *svātantrya* (*Spanda-Nirṇaya* on *Spanda-Kārikā* 1.1, the *unmeṣa-nimeṣa* verse). Crucially, *spanda* is the engine of *manifestation as a whole*: closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā* than to any single *guṇa*, and definitively not equatable with *rajas* (the gloss develops *unmeṣa-nimeṣābhyāṃ jagataḥ pralayodayau* at *SpN* 1.1 as covering the totality of cosmic emergence and dissolution; the *jagataḥ* is universal-scope, not gunic-scope). Kṣemarāja systematically integrates *Spanda* with *Pratyabhijñā*: the *spanda*-doctrine supplies the dynamism that the *Pratyabhijñā* idealism otherwise risks under-articulating; conversely the *Pratyabhijñā* supplies the analysis of subject-structure (*aham*, *vimarśa*, *ābhāsa*) that *Spanda* needs (*SpN* prefatory *bhūmikā*). The four *niṣyandas* of the original *Kārikā* are read as: the establishment of *spanda* (*SpK* / *SpN* 1.1–25), its operation in the bound subject (*SpK* / *SpN* 2.1–7), the practical *upāyas* (*SpK* / *SpN* 3.1–19), and the condition of full recognition (*SpK* / *SpN* 4.1–2). The text is the standard reference for *spanda*-doctrine in its developed form.

engaged_works.title: Spanda-Sandoha
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed before the Spanda-Nirṇaya as a focused commentary on the first Spanda-Kārikā.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored compact commentary on the first verse of the Spanda-Kārikā.
engaged_works.summary: A focused commentary on the first *kārikā* of the *Spanda-Kārikā* — *yasyonmeṣa-nimeṣābhyāṃ jagataḥ pralayodayau* ("by whose unfolding and folding the world dissolves and arises"; *Spanda-Kārikā* 1.1) — taken as a *bīja*-verse containing in compressed form the entire *spanda*-doctrine. Kṣemarāja unfolds the verse word by word, extracting the doctrine that *spanda* is the unbroken pulsation of *Cit* (consciousness) by which the manifest universe is continuously projected, sustained, and reabsorbed; *unmeṣa* and *nimeṣa* are not two opposed processes but the single rhythm of *cit*'s self-display and self-rest (*Spanda-Sandoha* on *SpK* 1.1, ad *unmeṣa-nimeṣa*). The text serves as the analytic propaedeutic to the longer *Spanda-Nirṇaya*: where the *Nirṇaya* commentates the entire *Kārikā*, the *Sandoha* establishes the foundational interpretive frame within which the rest is to be read (*Sandoha* opening *bhūmikā*). *Spanda* is here precisely the dynamism of manifestation as a whole — closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā* than to any single *guṇa*, and definitively not equatable with *rajas* (the gloss makes the totality-scope explicit at *Sandoha* on *SpK* 1.1, ad *jagataḥ pralayodayau*). The work is shorter and pedagogically more compact than the *Nirṇaya*, often used as an entry-text by the school's living tradition.

engaged_works.title: Stava-Chintamani-Tika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa's Stava-Cintāmaṇi.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary on Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa's *Stava-Cintāmaṇi* — a Kashmirian Śaiva *stotra* in approximately 120 verses to Śiva, treated by the Trika tradition as a doctrinally-saturated devotional poem. Kṣemarāja's *Ṭīkā* reads the *Stava* simultaneously as devotional address and as compressed *Pratyabhijñā* exposition: each verse is shown to articulate, in liturgical voice, an aspect of the recognition (*pratyabhijñā*) that the worshipping *aham* is itself the supreme *aham* of Śiva (*Stava-Cintāmaṇi-Ṭīkā* on *Stava-Cintāmaṇi* 1, the opening invocation, where Kṣemarāja establishes the identity-recognition reading-frame). The commentary thereby continues the integration of *bhakti* and *pratyabhijñā* exemplified in Utpaladeva's *Śiva-Stotrāvalī* (which Kṣemarāja also commented on); together these works demonstrate that strong non-dualism within the Trika tradition does not abolish *bhakti* but re-grounds it in self-recognition. The text is a key witness to how the school understood its own devotional literature — not as ornamental adjunct to philosophy but as a parallel mode of the same self-recognition, with the *camatkāra* of devotional surrender structurally identical to the *camatkāra* of *pratyabhijñā* (consistent with the *PH* sūtra 17 doctrine that *vikalpa-kṣaya* yields *cidānanda-lābha*).

engaged_works.title: Shiva-Sutra-Vimarshini
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on the Śiva-Sūtras.
engaged_works.summary: Kṣemarāja's commentary on the *Śiva-Sūtras* attributed to Vasugupta — the revealed scriptural foundation of the Trika lineage. The *Vimarśinī* organizes the seventy-seven sūtras into the three *unmeṣas* — *Śāmbhava* (*ŚS* 1.1–22), *Śākta* (*ŚS* 2.1–10), *Āṇava* (*ŚS* 3.1–45) — corresponding to the three *upāyas* of progressively descending subtlety. The first *unmeṣa* opens with *caitanyam ātmā* (*ŚS* 1.1, "the self is consciousness itself") and develops the immediate *Śāmbhava* path of recognition without method (*ŚSV* on *ŚS* 1.1–22); the second works through the *Śākta* path of attentive *vimarśa* on the rising and subsiding of cognitions (*ŚSV* on *ŚS* 2.1–10); the third treats the *Āṇava* path of breath, mantra, and meditative supports for those still bound to the limited subject (*ŚSV* on *ŚS* 3.1–45, including *ŚS* 3.5 *nāḍī-saṃhāra-bhūta-jaya-bhūta-kaivalya-bhūta-pṛthaktvāni* on yogic mastery). Throughout, Kṣemarāja reads each sūtra simultaneously as scriptural revelation, as Pratyabhijñā philosophy, and as practical instruction, making the *Vimarśinī* the standard integrative commentary on the school's foundational scripture. For comparative purposes, the work is the locus classicus for the Trika doctrine that scripture, philosophy, and practice are three faces of the single self-recognition of consciousness.
